brand.title: ESource AI Group (Production)
brand.subtitle: Knowledge Check — CTFS for Sales Managers: From Discovery to Deal

questions.question: What are the five financial functions that CTFS unifies into a single platform?
choices: Budgeting, contracting, forecasting, site payments, and participant payments
Budgeting, invoicing, compliance, site payments, and audit tracking
Contracting, forecasting, payments, reporting, and analytics
Budgeting, contracting, site payments, enrollment tracking, and compliance
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly right—those five modules share data and their unified architecture is CTFS's core differentiator.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review Slide 6 for the precise list of CTFS modules. The unified connection across budgeting, contracting, forecasting, site payments, and participant payments is what sets the platform apart from point solutions.

questions.question: According to the course, what is the primary market problem that CTFS solves?
choices: Trials are moving too slowly because sites lack payment visibility
Manual workflows and siloed tools create operational complexity, with delays costing up to $55,000 per day in late-stage trials
Pharma companies lack the budget to hire enough finance staff
Clinical trial data is not being properly stored in cloud systems
questions.feedback_correct: That's the core problem named on Slide 4—fragmented financial workflows create measurable business risk, not just operational inconvenience.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The market problem is not just slow trials or staffing gaps. Slide 4 identifies that fragmented financial systems across budgeting, contracting, forecasting, and payments produce specific, quantifiable delays that cost sponsors up to $55,000 per day.

questions.question: When will CTFS Site Payments sandbox be available, and what is the next concrete step a rep can offer a qualified prospect today?
choices: Q1 2026; general commercial availability
Q4 2025; early adopter program enrollment
Q4 2025; sandbox access with general availability in Q1 2026
Q2 2026; beta testing with selected sponsors
questions.feedback_correct: Perfect—Slide 9 makes clear that Site Payments sandbox is Q4 2025, the early adopter program is live now, and full commercial availability is Q1 2026.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 9 specifies the exact timeline for Site Payments. The early adopter program is live right now as a concrete next step a rep can offer today, with sandbox in Q4 2025 and general availability in Q1 2026.

questions.question: Which of the four CTFS personas fears missing milestones and managing fallout when sites are frustrated with payment delays?
choices: Reena, VP of IT Clinical
Andres, Digital Transformation Leader
Gilbert, Clinical Operations Director
Rachel, Site Clinical Operations Director
questions.feedback_correct: Correct—Gilbert's world centers on trial timelines and site relationships, and that specific fear is what makes his persona distinct.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Each persona has a different fear profile. Slide 14 identifies Gilbert as the Clinical Operations Director whose primary concern is hitting milestones and managing the relationship impact when sites experience payment delays and visibility gaps.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating Total Net Value in a CTFS UVP?
choices: Prospect Revenue Uplift plus Cost Savings divided by Solution Cost
Prospect Revenue Uplift plus Cost Savings minus Solution Cost
Cost Savings minus Solution Cost plus Implementation Time
Prospect Revenue Uplift multiplied by Cost Savings divided by Solution Cost
questions.feedback_correct: That's the exact formula on Slide 20—and it's the structure your reps must use before any first call.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 20 presents the precise formula: Total Net Value equals Prospect Revenue Uplift plus Cost Savings minus Solution Cost. This is the foundation for building a numbers-driven UVP.

questions.question: What is the most important one-sentence output a manager should require from a rep before a first call?
choices: A competitive analysis explaining why CTFS beats GreenPhire and Medidata
A pitch template that names the target persona, specific metric, percentage improvement, and dollar savings in a defined timeframe
A timeline of all CTFS modules coming to market in the next 18 months
A list of all four personas and which one the prospect is most likely to be
questions.feedback_correct: Slide 22 calls this the single most important output—a sentence with the structure: 'We help [Target Persona] in [Industry] increase [Specific Metric] by [Percentage] and reduce [Pain Point] by [Dollar Amount] in [Timeframe].'
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 22 identifies the one-sentence pitch as the critical deliverable. Every blank in that sentence requires account research—if a rep cannot fill it with real numbers and persona language, they do not know the account yet.

questions.question: According to Slide 25, what should reps always ask about in discovery, and why does it matter for ROI calculations?
choices: The prospect's current software budget and annual IT spending
The prospect's projected annual revenue, because delay costs can dwarf everything else in the calculator
The number of trial sites the prospect currently manages globally
The prospect's expected timeline to full digital transformation maturity
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly—Slide 25 emphasizes that reps must ask for projected revenue per year, because even a modest delay cost multiplied by blockbuster drug revenue (potentially $1 million per day) can far outweigh implementation costs.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 25 highlights that projected annual revenue is the critical number to ask for early. When you divide annual revenue by 365, you get daily delay cost—and for blockbuster drugs, this single factor can dwarf all other ROI components.

questions.question: Why does the course emphasize that Digital Transformation Leaders fear proof-of-concept pilots that cannot scale, and how should a rep position CTFS to address that fear?
choices: Because digital budgets are often questioned at the board level; position CTFS as proof that all innovation pilots succeed
Because the innovation budget is at risk if transformation stalls; position CTFS as the unified orchestration layer that proves ROI and scales beyond proof-of-concept
Because Andres lacks technical credibility; position CTFS as a simple platform that requires no technical integration
Because most competitors have failed; position CTFS as the only platform that has ever succeeded in enterprise scale
questions.feedback_correct: That's exactly what Slide 16 names—Andres fears stalled pilots and board-level ROI questioning, and the positioning is that CTFS provides the unified orchestration layer that moves transformation beyond proof-of-concept into enterprise impact.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 16 identifies that Andres's core fear is wasting the innovation budget on a platform that cannot scale or demonstrate value to the board. Position CTFS not as the only solution, but as the unified orchestration layer that enables transformation to move beyond pilot into enterprise-wide impact.

questions.question: When a rep is in early discovery and the prospect's own financial data is not yet available, what should they do to build a credible cost savings calculation?
choices: Tell the prospect they must provide their own data before any conversation can continue
Use CTFS-specific benchmarks like up to 50% reduction in financial processing time and $3M annual savings for high-volume sponsors to establish a credible starting point
Assume a standard 30% cost reduction across all prospects in that industry vertical
Wait until the second meeting when the prospect has gathered their internal financial data
questions.feedback_correct: Perfect—Slide 21 provides specific CTFS benchmarks that give reps a credible anchor before prospect data is available, and it positions the rep as knowledgeable without requiring the prospect to lead the conversation.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 21 emphasizes that benchmarks serve two purposes: they give the rep a credible starting point and they demonstrate CTFS's market track record. This builds momentum in early discovery before you have access to the prospect's own numbers.

questions.question: How should a rep handle the question of delivery model—SaaS versus TES versus hybrid—and what is the key coaching principle a manager should reinforce?
choices: Always recommend SaaS first because it is the simplest and cheapest option for any organization

SaaS fits large sponsors with capable teams; TES fits organizations needing managed services or lacking internal capacity; the manager should coach reps to match delivery model to the prospect's team capacity and size, not just close the deal quickly
Recommend TES for all early-stage deals because it generates more recurring revenue
The delivery model is determined by the prospect's IT department, so reps should defer that decision entirely to technical conversations
questions.feedback_correct: Slide 10 outlines the three models and their fit criteria. The coaching principle is that delivery model must match prospect reality—large, capable teams can run SaaS; smaller or less-resourced organizations benefit from TES; and some need hybrid.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Slide 10 clarifies that delivery model is a strategic decision, not a technical one. SaaS, TES, and hybrid each fit different prospect profiles. A manager's job is to coach reps to match delivery model to team capacity and organizational size, ensuring the prospect can actually operate the solution they buy.
